content: Find the rate of disappearance of $B$ for the reaction $2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$, from following.

options.content: twice the rate of C is formed

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &2 A+B \rightarrow 2 C+D\\ &\text { Rate of reaction }\\ &=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{~A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{~B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{D}]}{\mathrm{dt}} \end{aligned} $$

Rate of disappearance of B

$=$ half the rate as A disappears

$=$ half the rate as C is formed

$=$ same rate as D is formed

content:
For the reaction

$$ \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} $$

$\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ disappears at the rate of $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Find the rate of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ formation.
explanation:
For given reaction,

$$ \mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} $$

rate of reaction

$$ =-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]}{\mathrm{dt}} $$

Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{N}_2$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & =\frac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ \therefore & \frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \times\left(-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right) \\ & =2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ & =4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \end{array} $$

content: Half life of a first order reaction is 3 hours. Calculate the value of $\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_0}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}$ after 8 hours.
explanation:
For $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction,

$$ k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{3}=0.231 \mathrm{~h}^{-1} $$

Integrated rate law for $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log _{10} \frac{[A]_0}{[A]_t} \\ & 0.231=\frac{2.303}{8} \log _{10} \frac{[A]_0}{[A]_t} \\ & \log _{10} \frac{[A]_0}{[A]_t}=\frac{8 \times 0.231}{2.303}=0.8024 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{[A]_0}{[A]_t}=\text { Antilog }(0.8024)=6.34 \end{array} $$

content: $60 \%$ of a first order reaction is completed in 60 minute. What is the time taken by reactant to decompose half of its original amount?
explanation:
The integrated rate law for the first order reaction is $\mathrm{k}=\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10} \frac{[\mathrm{~A}]_0}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & {[\mathrm{A}]_0=100 \%,[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}=100-60=40 \%} \\ & \mathrm{t}=60 \mathrm{~min} \\ & \mathrm{k}=\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{t}} \log _{10} \frac{100}{40} \\ & \quad=\frac{2.303}{60 \mathrm{~min}} \log _{10}(2.5) \\ & \quad=\frac{2.303}{60 \mathrm{~min}} \times 0.3979 \\ & =0.01527 \\ & \approx 0.0153 \mathrm{~min}^{-1} \\ & \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{0.693}{0.0153 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}} \\ & =45.29 \approx 45.3 \mathrm{~min} \end{aligned} $$

content:
Consider the reaction

$$ 3 \mathrm{I}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_{8(\mathrm{aq})}^{--} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{4(\mathrm{aq})}^{2-} $$

rate of formation of $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ at a particular time is

$$ 2.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

What is the value of $\frac{d}{d t}\left[I_3^{-}\right]$?

The rate law for the reaction, $x \mathrm{~A}+y \mathrm{~B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{mP}+\mathrm{nQ}$, is $\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{c}} \times[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{d}}$ What is the overall order of reaction?
explanation:
Given:

Reaction:

$ x\,A + y\,B \rightarrow m\,P + n\,Q $

Rate law:

$ r = k [A]^c [B]^d $

To find:

Overall order of reaction

Concept:

The overall order of a reaction is the sum of the powers of the concentration terms in its rate law.

That is:

$ \text{Overall order} = c + d $

Therefore:

The correct answer is:

✅ Option C: $ (c + d) $

content: What is the order of a reaction having unit of the rate constant $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ ?
explanation:
Given:

Unit of the rate constant is $\mathrm{mol}\ \mathrm{dm}^{-3}\ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$.

Step 1: General formula for rate constant units

For a reaction of order $n$, units of rate constant $k$ are:

$$ [\text{Rate}] = [k] [\text{Concentration}]^n $$

The rate of reaction has units:

$$ \text{mol}\ \text{dm}^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1} $$

The concentration has units:

$$ \text{mol}\ \text{dm}^{-3} $$

So,

$$ [k] = \frac{[\text{Rate}]}{[\text{Concentration}]^n} = \frac{\text{mol}\ \text{dm}^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}}{(\text{mol}\ \text{dm}^{-3})^n} $$

Step 2: Simplify units

$$ [k] = \text{mol}^{1-n}\ \text{dm}^{-3(1-n)}\ \text{s}^{-1} $$

Step 3: Set given unit equal to the derived unit

Given unit: $\text{mol}\ \text{dm}^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}$

So,

$$ \text{mol}^{1-n}\ \text{dm}^{-3(1-n)}\ \text{s}^{-1} = \text{mol}^1\ \text{dm}^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1} $$

Step 4: Equate the powers for $\text{mol}$ and $\text{dm}^{-3}$

For $\text{mol}$:

$$ 1-n = 1 \implies n = 0 $$

For $\text{dm}^{-3}$:

$$ -3(1-n) = -3 \implies 1-n = 1 \implies n = 0 $$

So, the order of reaction is $0$.

Final Answer:

Option A: 0

content: What is rate constant of a first order reaction having half life 138.6 minute?
explanation:
For a first order reaction, the relationship between half-life ($t_{1/2}$) and rate constant ($k$) is:

$$ t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k} $$

Given: $t_{1/2} = 138.6$ minutes.

Let's solve for $k$:

Step 1: Write the formula.

$$ k = \frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}} $$

Step 2: Substitute the value.

$$ k = \frac{0.693}{138.6} $$

Step 3: Calculate the value.

$$ k = 0.005 \ \text{minute}^{-1} $$

Correct Option:

Option D: $0.005 \ \text{minute}^{-1}$

content:
For the reaction,

$$ 3 \mathrm{I}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_{8(\mathrm{aq})}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4(\mathrm{aq})}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} $$

rate of formation of $\mathrm{SO}_{4(\text { aq })}^{2-}$ is $0.044 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.

What is the rate of consumption of $\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_{8(\mathrm{aq})}^{2-}$ ?
explanation:
First, write the balanced chemical equation:

$$ 3 \mathrm{I}^{-} (aq) + \mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_8^{2-} (aq) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-} (aq) + \mathrm{I}_3^{-} (aq) $$

According to the law of rate of reaction:

The rate of the reaction can be written as:

$$ \text{Rate} = -\frac{1}{3} \frac{d[\mathrm{I}^{-}]}{dt} = -\frac{d[\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_8^{2-}]}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{d[\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}]}{dt} = \frac{d[\mathrm{I}_3^{-}]}{dt} $$

We are given:

$$ \text{Rate of formation of } \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-} = \frac{d[\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}]}{dt} = 0.044 \ \mathrm{mol~dm}^{-3}\mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

Now, use the stoichiometric relation:

$$ \text{Rate} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{d[\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}]}{dt} $$

So,

$$ \text{Rate} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.044 = 0.022 \ \mathrm{mol~dm}^{-3}\mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

The rate of consumption of $\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_8^{2-}$ is:

$$ \left| \frac{d[\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_8^{2-}]}{dt} \right| = \text{Rate} = 0.022\ \mathrm{mol~dm}^{-3}\mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

Answer:

Option A $0.022 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ is correct.

content: Which of the following equation exhibits integrated rate law equation for first order reaction?
explanation:
The integrated rate law for a first-order reaction is:

$$\ln\frac{[A]_0}{[A]} = kt$$

or, converting to base-10 logarithms,

$$k = \frac{2.303}{t}\,\log\frac{[A]_0}{[A]}\,. $$

If we let

• $a=[A]_0$ (initial concentration)

• $a-x=[A]$ (concentration at time $t$)

then the correct expression is

$$k = \frac{2.303}{t}\,\log\frac{a}{\,a - x\,}\,, $$

which corresponds to Option B.

Quick check of the others:

• Option C has the wrong sign (it would give $k<0$).

• Option D is the integrated law for a second-order reaction.

• Option A isn’t in the form of any standard integrated rate law.

content: Calculate activation energy for a reaction if it's rate doubles when temperature is raised from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $35^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
If half life of a first order reaction is 10 minutes, find the time required to decrease concentration of reactant from 0.08 M to 0.02 M .
explanation:
For a first‐order reaction the half‐life $t_{1/2}$ is related to the rate constant $k$ by

$$t_{1/2}=\frac{\ln2}{k}$$

Given $t_{1/2}=10\,$min,

$$k=\frac{\ln2}{10\ {\rm min}}$$

Also the integrated rate law for a first‐order reaction is

$$\ln\frac{[A]_t}{[A]_0}=-kt$$

Here $[A]_0=0.08\,$M and $[A]_t=0.02\,$M. So,

Compute $k$:

$$k=\frac{\ln2}{10}$$

Write the time expression:

$$t=\frac{1}{k}\,\ln\frac{[A]_0}{[A]_t}$$

Substitute values:

$$t=\frac{1}{(\ln2/10)}\,\ln\frac{0.08}{0.02} =\frac{10}{\ln2}\,\ln4$$

But $\ln4=2\ln2$, so

$$t=\frac{10}{\ln2}\times2\ln2=20\ {\rm min}$$

Alternatively, since each half‐life of 10 min halves the concentration:

0.08 M → 0.04 M in 10 min

0.04 M → 0.02 M in another 10 min

Total time = 10 min + 10 min = 20 min.

Answer: Option B (20 minutes).
